worth_it_explanation: Inventory that reaches children carries legal obligations under the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA), and a publisher who monetizes that inventory through Equativ controls whether personalized ads and certain data uses are suppressed on it. A misclassified placement can run personalized advertising on a child-directed page, which exposes the publisher to enforcement and pushes the risk down the supply chain to demand partners. The check is a review of how child-directed inventory is flagged and how restricted data processing is applied in a setup the publisher already runs, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete configuration the publisher can correct.
intro: When a publisher monetizes inventory through Equativ, any placement that is directed to children or likely to reach them brings COPPA into scope, which limits the data that may be collected and how ads may be targeted. This subcategory is about whether that inventory is flagged so personalized ads and certain data uses are suppressed on it, and whether restricted data processing reaches the placements where minors are likely to appear. These checks confirm that the child-directed treatment is a deliberate configuration the publisher can point to rather than a default that was never set, and that the suppression follows the inventory the law actually covers.

audit_checks.title: Child-directed inventory is not flagged to suppress personalized ads
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a child-directed placement is not flagged, personalized advertising and the data collection behind it can run on inventory the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) covers, which exposes the publisher to direct enforcement and carries the risk to every demand partner that buys the impression. The harm reaches a regulated audience and is hard to undo after the fact, so an unflagged child-directed placement is a serious configuration gap rather than a routine hygiene item.

educate.base:
The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act, or COPPA, is a United States law that limits the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information from children under 13. It reaches a site or service directed to children, a general-audience service that has actual knowledge it is collecting information from a child, and the third-party services, e.g., ad networks, that collect information on those properties. For a publisher monetizing inventory through Equativ, the placements directed to children sit squarely inside that scope, and the personalized advertising that normally drives revenue is one of the data uses the law constrains.

Suppressing personalized ads on this inventory is the mechanism that keeps the monetization inside the law. A child-directed placement should serve non-personalized advertising, which contextual ads chosen from the page content rather than from a profile built on the visitor, and it should not feed the visitor's data into audience building, retargeting, or the identifiers that personalized advertising depends on. The publisher's side of this is to flag the inventory so the suppression travels with every impression rather than depending on each demand partner to guess.

The flag has to follow the inventory the law covers, which is broader than a section labeled 'for kids'. A general-audience property can hold child-directed pages, and a placement that mixes audiences still needs the treatment if children are a meaningful part of who lands there. A publisher who has not mapped which placements are directed to children, or who relies on a single site-wide setting that does not match the page-level reality, can leave a child-directed placement serving personalized ads without anyone seeing it.

A few signs point to child-directed inventory that is not flagged:
  • No record exists of which placements are directed to children or likely to reach them in meaningful numbers
  • Personalized advertising runs across all inventory with no child-directed suppression set on the placements that should carry it
  • The publisher cannot say how a child-directed flag is set or whether it reaches the page-level inventory rather than only a site-wide default
You may want to confirm that every child-directed placement is flagged so personalized advertising is suppressed on it. One approach is to map the inventory against the audience each placement actually reaches, then check that the suppression is set on each placement the map identifies as child-directed.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a children's section, a games or activities area, or an education vertical can be directed to children even when the rest of the property is general-audience. You may want to map those sections against the audience each one reaches, then confirm the child-directed flag is set at the placement level for them rather than relying on a single site-wide setting that the mixed-audience property does not fit.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a toy, kids' apparel, or children's product category can reach a child-directed audience even though the storefront overall serves adults making purchases. You may want to check whether those category pages are flagged so personalized advertising is suppressed on them, rather than treating the whole property as general-audience because the buyer is usually an adult.

learn_more.source_title: Complying with COPPA: Frequently Asked Questions
Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: Not Just for Kids' Sites

impactful_updates.update_summary: The Federal Trade Commission finalized amendments to the COPPA Rule that require operators to obtain separate verifiable parental consent before disclosing a child's personal information to third parties for targeted advertising. A publisher monetizing child-directed inventory cannot treat personalized advertising on that inventory as available by default.

investigate.intro: Review which placements are directed to children or likely to reach them, and check whether each is flagged so personalized advertising is suppressed rather than relying on a site-wide default.

inventory_map.label: Inventory and audience review
steps: List the placements monetized through Equativ and, for each, note the page or section it sits on and the audience that section is meant for.
Mark which placements are directed to children or are likely to reach children in meaningful numbers, using the page content and the audience rather than only a section label.
For each marked placement, record whether a child-directed flag or non-personalized-ads setting is applied to it specifically or only to a broader site-wide default.
Ask the publisher how the child-directed treatment is set in the Equativ console and whether it reaches placement-level inventory.

ad_inspection.label: Served-ad inspection
steps: Load a sample of the child-directed placements and inspect the ads that serve, looking for signs of personalized or retargeted creative rather than contextual ads.
Check whether identifiers or audience signals are being read on those pages, e.g., a personalized-ad request in the network calls, which would indicate the suppression is not in force.
Record any child-directed placement where personalized advertising or its underlying data collection appears to be running.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per placement, whether it is directed to children and whether a child-directed flag suppressing personalized advertising is applied to it. A setup where every child-directed placement carries the suppression at the placement level passes. A setup where a child-directed placement runs personalized advertising, or relies on a site-wide default that does not reach the page-level inventory, is a fail recorded with the placements affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which placements are child-directed and whether each suppresses personalized advertising above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} serves personalized advertising on inventory that is directed to children without a child-directed flag suppressing it. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) limits the data collection and ad targeting allowed on child-directed inventory, so a placement that reaches children should serve non-personalized, contextual ads rather than ads built on a visitor profile. {{voice_recommend}} mapping which placements are directed to children, then setting the child-directed suppression at the placement level in Equativ so personalized advertising and its underlying data collection are turned off on each one. After the change, inspect the ads serving on those placements to confirm the suppression is in force.

audit_checks.title: Restricted data processing is not applied to inventory likely to reach minors
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Restricted data processing limits how a visitor's data is used on inventory that is likely to reach minors, and when it is not applied, identifiers and audience signals can be collected from that inventory in ways the publisher cannot defend if questioned. The gap sits on inventory that is not clearly child-directed but still reaches minors, so the exposure is real without being as direct as serving personalized ads on a known child-directed page.

educate.base:
Restricted data processing is the treatment that limits how a visitor's data is collected and used so that an impression stays inside what the law allows. On inventory likely to reach minors, it means the impression is handled without the identifiers, audience profiling, and cross-site signals that personalized advertising depends on, even where the placement is not formally labeled child-directed. For a publisher monetizing through Equativ, applying this treatment is how inventory in the gray area between general-audience and child-directed is kept defensible.

The reason this matters separately from the child-directed flag is that the two cover different inventory. A child-directed flag handles the placements a publisher has determined are directed to children. Restricted data processing reaches further, to the inventory that is not clearly child-directed but still draws a meaningful share of minors, e.g., a family-oriented section, a how-to vertical that attracts students, or a general page that a popular children's property links into. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) turns on actual knowledge of collecting information from a child, so a publisher who knows a placement reaches minors carries an obligation there even without a kids' label.

The signal also has to travel. When the publisher applies restricted data processing, the privacy signal needs to pass to demand partners so they handle the impression the same way, which on a Prebid-compatible platform such as Equativ flows through the Global Privacy Platform (GPP) string that carries the consent and processing state to bidders. A treatment set on the publisher's side that does not reach the bid request leaves demand partners processing the data as if no restriction applied.

A few signs point to restricted data processing that is not applied where it should be:
  • Inventory likely to reach minors carries no restricted data processing, with the publisher treating only formally labeled child-directed placements as in scope
  • The privacy signal that should carry the restriction is absent from the bid request, e.g., no GPP string passing the processing state to demand partners
  • The publisher cannot say which placements draw a meaningful share of minors or how the restriction is meant to apply to them
You may want to confirm that restricted data processing reaches the inventory likely to draw minors, not only the placements formally labeled child-directed. One approach is to identify those placements from the audience the content attracts, apply the restriction to them, and verify the privacy signal carrying it reaches the bid request.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a family or parenting section, a homework-help vertical, or coverage of a children's franchise can draw a meaningful share of minors without being formally a kids' section. You may want to apply restricted data processing to those placements based on the audience they actually attract, then confirm the privacy signal carrying the restriction reaches demand partners.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, gift guides, family-activity content, or pages tied to children's products can draw minors alongside the adult buyers. You may want to extend restricted data processing to that inventory rather than limiting it to pages explicitly built for children, and check that the restriction passes through to the bid request.

learn_more.source_title: Complying with COPPA: Frequently Asked Questions

investigate.intro: Review which placements are likely to reach minors, check whether restricted data processing is applied to them, and confirm the privacy signal carrying that restriction reaches the bid request.

audience_review.label: Audience and inventory review
steps: List the placements monetized through Equativ and identify which ones draw a meaningful share of minors from the content and the audience, beyond the placements formally labeled child-directed.
For each, record whether restricted data processing is applied and how the publisher set it.
Note any placement likely to reach minors where no restriction is applied because it was not treated as in scope.

signal_inspection.label: Privacy-signal inspection
steps: On a sample of the placements likely to reach minors, inspect the bid request and confirm a privacy signal carrying the processing state is present, e.g., a Global Privacy Platform (GPP) string in the request to demand partners.
Check whether the signal reflects the restriction the publisher intends rather than a default that passes no restriction.
Record any placement where the restriction is set on the publisher's side but the signal carrying it is missing from the bid request.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per placement likely to reach minors, whether restricted data processing is applied and whether the privacy signal carrying it reaches the bid request. A setup where the restriction reaches the inventory likely to draw minors and the signal passes to demand partners passes. A setup where that inventory carries no restriction, or where the restriction is set but the signal is absent from the bid request, is a fail recorded with the placements affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which placements are likely to reach minors and whether restricted data processing and its privacy signal are applied above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not apply restricted data processing to inventory likely to reach minors, treating only formally labeled child-directed placements as in scope. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) turns on actual knowledge that a placement reaches children, so inventory that draws a meaningful share of minors carries an obligation even without a kids' label, and the restriction has to reach the bid request to bind demand partners. {{voice_recommend}} identifying the placements likely to draw minors from the audience the content attracts, applying restricted data processing to them, and confirming the Global Privacy Platform (GPP) signal carrying that restriction reaches the bid request. After the change, inspect a sample of bid requests on those placements to confirm the signal is present and reflects the restriction.
